text:
Feijo, Diego 12:06 PM
I would like your opinions about a theory. While researching this problem, I read
(https://www.mongodb.com/docs/drivers/node/current/connect/connection-options/connection-pools/#avoid-socket-timeouts)
that larger connection pools could not be beneficial in scenarios where we have a light workload (since we don't have a large
number of requests reading/writing all the time). When we have a lot of open sockets idle, they will be recycled often. Then the
client/server will have the burden of reestablishing a lot of connections frequently. To test that theory, I drastically reduced the
limit in the connection pool. I deployed the changes last night, and it has been running for 10 hours now with not a single failure.
I changed the settings as follows:
• JavaScript
•..
/** @type {MongoClientOptions} */
const mongoClientOptions = {
// Connection establishment
connectTimeoutMS: 10_000, |/ Time in ms to connect to the database before timing out
serverSelectionTimeoutMS: 10_000, // Time in ms to wait for a suitable server in the cluster be
// Socket behavior
socketTimeoutMS: 60_000, // Time in ms to wait for a response from the server before closing th
// Pool tuning for light workloads
maxPoolSize: 5, // No need for a large pool with light workloads
minPoolSize: 1, I/ Keep at least one connection warm
maxIdleTimeMS: 300_000, |/ Prevents constant teardowns/reconnect
waitQueueTimeoutMS: 30_000, I/ Time in ms that a thread waits for a connection from the pool be
// Resiliency
retryReads: true,
retrywrites: true,
}
